description: Academic researcher writing a paper on privacy-preserving federated learning, approaching a conference deadline
persona: PhD student in computer science, 3rd year. Research focus: differential privacy and federated learning. Collaborating with a cryptographer. Submitting to USENIX Security.

memories.content: Starting research on differential privacy for federated learning. Reading McMahan et al. (2017) FedAvg paper as the primary baseline.
memories.label: Federated learning research started

memories.content: Advisor meeting: paper topic approved - Privacy Guarantees in Federated Learning Without Centralized Data. Scope confirmed.
memories.label: Paper topic approved by advisor

memories.content: Read Dwork et al. (2006) foundational differential privacy paper. Epsilon-delta formalization is the key theoretical anchor for the threat model.
memories.label: Dwork et al. DP paper read

memories.content: Cornell arxiv has useful recent work on local DP vs central DP tradeoffs. Saved 12 papers to the reading list for literature review.
memories.label: Local vs central DP reading list

memories.content: Bonawitz et al. (2017) on Practical Secure Aggregation - clients never share raw gradients with the server. Key paper for the related work section.
memories.label: Bonawitz secure aggregation paper

memories.content: Introduced to Dr. Reyes, a cryptographer who can review the secure aggregation proofs in the paper. Will meet bi-weekly.
memories.label: Dr. Reyes - cryptography collaborator

memories.content: Literature review draft complete - 47 papers reviewed, 22 cited in the current draft. Related work section covers DP-SGD, secure aggregation, and non-IID distribution.
memories.label: Literature review draft complete

memories.content: Experiment 1 design: compare DP-SGD with varying epsilon values (0.1, 1.0, 10.0) against a non-private baseline on CIFAR-10.
memories.label: Experiment 1: DP-SGD epsilon sweep

memories.content: Dr. Reyes identified a flaw in the threat model - the passive adversary assumption is too strong for real federated deployments where clients may collude.
memories.label: Threat model flaw flagged by Dr. Reyes

memories.content: USENIX Security submission deadline is 8 weeks away. The results section must be complete before then to allow revision time.
memories.label: USENIX deadline: 8 weeks out

memories.content: DP-SGD experiments running on the university cluster. First results: epsilon=0.1 causes 14% accuracy loss. Epsilon=1.0 causes 4% loss. Acceptable tradeoff.
memories.label: DP-SGD experiment results

memories.content: Revised threat model to active adversary who can observe k colluding clients. Dr. Reyes reviewed the revised section and approved the formulation.
memories.label: Threat model revised to active adversary

memories.content: USENIX deadline extended 2 weeks - now 10 weeks out. Added breathing room to design a second experiment comparing FedAvg vs FedProx on non-IID data.
memories.label: USENIX deadline extended 2 weeks

memories.content: Writing the methodology section - struggling to explain the aggregation protocol clearly without equations becoming walls of notation. Need better diagrams.
memories.label: Methodology section writing difficulty

memories.content: Created system diagram for the aggregation protocol. Dr. Reyes reviewed and suggested simplifying step 3 to remove an unnecessary round trip.
memories.label: Aggregation diagram reviewed by Dr. Reyes

memories.content: FedProx vs FedAvg results on non-IID splits: FedProx converges 23% faster and achieves 2.1% higher accuracy. Strong enough result to include in the paper.
memories.label: FedProx vs FedAvg experiment results

memories.content: Results section: 4 of 6 subsections complete. Need to write the discussion of accuracy-privacy tradeoffs and the conclusion.
memories.label: Results section 4 of 6 complete

memories.content: A USENIX PC member reached out asking whether we plan to open-source the DP-SGD implementation. Confirmed yes - will release with camera-ready.
memories.label: USENIX reviewer: open-source inquiry

memories.content: Discussion section first draft complete. Sent to Dr. Reyes for review of the cryptographic claims - especially the active adversary bound proof.
memories.label: Discussion draft sent to Dr. Reyes

memories.content: Paper is at 14 pages. USENIX page limit is 13. Need to cut one page, targeting the related work section which has redundant citations.
memories.label: Paper over page limit: need to cut

memories.content: Trimmed related work section - removed 6 peripheral citations and tightened two paragraphs. Paper now at 13.4 pages. Almost there.
memories.label: Related work trimmed to 13.4 pages

goals.content: Goal: submit the federated learning privacy paper to USENIX Security on time and in good shape - this is the primary PhD milestone for the year.
goals.label: Submit to USENIX Security

goals.content: Goal: open-source the DP-SGD implementation alongside the camera-ready paper submission.
goals.label: Open-source code release with paper

questions.question: What is the current status of the paper and what still needs to be done before submission?
What experiments have I run and what were the key results?
What is my collaboration with Dr. Reyes and how has it shaped the paper?
What are the immediate deadlines and what needs to happen before they pass?
What do I know about differential privacy techniques from my research?
